meta.notes: First real brief, generated by hand to prove the pipeline end-to-end. Personal context (calendar + inbox) is off until those connectors are authorized. From here the morning routine produces this automatically.

sections.title: Today, in one line
sections.text: Two homes of yours had a consequential week: the 227th General Assembly just reset where the PC(USA) puts its moral weight, and Philadelphia began pouring $100M into its neighborhoods for America's 250th — including grants your own church could claim.

sections.title: Faith & Ministry
sections.summary: The 227th General Assembly has adjourned, and its decisions will shape denominational advocacy for years.

items.headline: General Assembly declares the war in Gaza a genocide and divests
items.body: Meeting in Milwaukee through July 2, the 227th General Assembly voted 454–15 to name Israel's war in Gaza a genocide and to lobby Congress for an arms embargo, days after unanimously divesting from Palantir and GE Aerospace. Assembly staff say the actions give the denomination's advocacy offices new clarity on everything from Christian nationalism to gun violence and poverty.
items.why: However you read the substance, this is now your denomination's official posture — worth understanding before it comes up in session or coffee hour.

items.headline: A quieter, structural step on LGBTQIA+ inclusion
items.body: Commissioners approved an optional feature letting congregations signal openness to calling an LGBTQIA+ pastor through the Ministry Discernment Profile, and advanced a sweeping new study on sexuality and family. Less a headline than a slow reshaping of how congregations and candidates find each other.

items.headline: Still more than a million members
items.body: Amid the decline story, the PC(USA) reported total 2025 membership of 1,019,003 — still a denomination of over a million people. A useful number to hold next to the anxiety.

items.headline: Proper 10 (Year A): the Parable of the Sower
items.body: Sunday, July 12 brings Matthew 13:1–9, 18–23 — the sower and the soils — paired with Jacob and Esau (Genesis 25:19–34), Romans 8:1–11, and Psalm 119:105–112. A rich week for anyone preaching or reflecting on what makes a word take root.

sections.title: Arts & Culture
sections.summary: From the Whitney's open collection — one thing to see, one to sit with.

items.headline: ‘Andy Warhol: Family Album’ and the 2026 Biennial, both up now
items.body: The Whitney is showing Andy Warhol: Family Album (through Oct 19) alongside the Whitney Biennial 2026 (through Aug 23) and Kelly Akashi's Hyundai Terrace Commission. A strong summer if a New York day materializes.

items.headline: Alma Thomas, ‘Mars Dust’ (1972)
items.body: A dense field of vertical red strokes over deep blue, made the year NASA's Mariner 9 was mapping Mars. Thomas spent 35 years teaching art in D.C. public schools and had her first major museum recognition in her eighties — a life that rhymes with leadership that ripens slowly and gives itself away. On view now.
items.why: A patient, generative life in the arts — the kind of soul your own work keeps pointing toward.

image.credit: Alma Thomas, Mars Dust, 1972. Acrylic on canvas, 69¼ × 57⅛ in. Whitney Museum of American Art, New York; purchase, with funds from The Hament Corporation 72.58. © Estate of Alma Thomas (Courtesy of the Hart Family) / Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York.

sections.title: Community & Civic
sections.summary: Philadelphia is spending big on neighborhoods ahead of the nation's 250th — some of it claimable.

items.headline: The city commits $100M to neighborhoods for the Semiquincentennial
items.body: Philadelphia is directing $100 million toward neighborhood initiatives for America's 250th birthday, explicitly aiming to spread the benefits to every neighborhood rather than center-city set pieces — community events, local businesses, and public art.

items.headline: Neighborhood Celebration Grants: $25K–$100K to local nonprofits
items.body: The city launched a $1M Neighborhood Celebration Grant Program offering $25,000–$100,000 to local nonprofits for community events, arts exhibitions, performances, historic commemorations, and beautification. Mural Arts is also painting 20 neighborhood-designed bells.
items.why: This is concretely claimable — Overbrook Presbyterian or a partner could fit a block celebration, commemoration, or arts project squarely inside these criteria.

items.headline: In Kensington, a soccer club that is really a refuge
items.body: NPR profiled a Kensington youth soccer club giving kids a safe place to simply play, in a neighborhood long shaped by crime and the drug trade — small, unglamorous, durable community-building of exactly the kind that lasts.

sections.title: Tech, AI & Tools
sections.summary: Signal, not noise — what actually changed and what it might be good for.

items.headline: Claude ‘Cowork’ comes to web and mobile — and keeps running when you're offline
items.body: Anthropic extended its Cowork agent (a delegate-a-task assistant, distinct from coding) to web and mobile for Max subscribers. You can hand it work from a desk, check status from your phone, and collect the result later — it continues even when none of your devices are online.
items.why: This is roughly the shape of what's assembling this brief — worth knowing as you think about delegating the recurring 'work around work.'

items.headline: AI is quietly moving into science, government, and mission work
items.body: Anthropic also launched Claude Science (a research workbench) and put Claude into a FedRAMP-High government environment this month. The through-line: these tools are leaving the developer's terminal for domains — research, public service, nonprofits — closer to yours.

items.headline: There may be a habitable-zone world just 10.7 light-years away
items.body: Astronomers reported GJ 887 d, a super-Earth orbiting a nearby red dwarf inside its habitable zone — the second-nearest known potentially-habitable exoplanet after Proxima b. Close enough, in cosmic terms, to be almost a neighbor.

sections.title: For the Radar
sections.summary: Worth watching, not acting on today.

items.headline: A new PC(USA) study on sexuality and family
items.body: Commissioned this Assembly; likely to shape denominational conversation for the next cycle.

items.headline: Philadelphia's summer of mega-events
items.body: FIFA World Cup matches, the MLB All-Star Game, and the Semiquincentennial converge on the city — traffic, tourism, and civic strain to plan around.

items.headline: U.S. lifts export controls on Anthropic's top models
items.body: Commerce removed restrictions on Fable 5 and Mythos 5; a small marker of shifting AI policy.

items.headline: Vasco da Gama sets sail for India
items.body: On this day, da Gama left Lisbon on the first direct European sea voyage to India — a departure that redrew the map of trade and contact, for better and for much worse. A fitting date to remember that setting out changes more than the one who sets out.

sections.text: The place God calls you to is the place where your deep gladness and the world's deep hunger meet.
